sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 36, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 35 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After SPACE:
- White extension expanded to cols 16-20 (node 1: W, pixels=16, boundary=[[28, 16], [28, 20], [31, 20], [31, 16]])
- Green body at cols 21-23 (node 2)
- Left blue square frame still at cols 24-27 (node 3)
- Left blue center at cols 17-18 (node 5) - this is INSIDE the white extension now!
- Carried blue center at cols 25-26 (node 6) - this is INSIDE the left blue square frame
So SPACE extended the white part to the LEFT (cols 16-20), and the left blue center at cols 17-18 is now inside the white extension. This means I DID collect the left blue center!
But wait, the left blue square frame is still there at cols 24-27. And the carried blue center from the right square is at cols 25-26, inside the frame. So it seems like:
1. I collected the left blue center (now inside white extension at cols 17-18)
2. The left blue square frame is still there
3. The carried blue center from the right square is inside the left blue square frame
So the mechanic is: SPACE extends the white part in the direction the player is facing (the side where the white extension is). The blue center that was inside the frame gets "pulled" into the white extension.
Now I have two blue centers collected:
1. Left blue center at cols 17-18 (inside white extension)
2. Right blue center at cols 25-26 (inside the left blue square frame)
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the segmentation:
- Node 5: b, pixels=4, boundary=[[29, 17], [29, 18], [30, 18], [30, 17]] - this is the left blue center
- Node 6: b, pixels=4, boundary=[[29, 25], [29, 26], [30, 26], [30, 25]] - this is the carried blue center from the right square
The white extension is at cols 16-20. The left blue center at cols 17-18 is inside the white extension. The carried blue center at cols 25-26 is inside the left blue square frame (cols 24-27).
So it seems like when I pressed SPACE, the white extension expanded to the left, collecting the left blue center. The carried blue center from the right square stayed where it was (inside the left blue square frame).
Now I need to collect the center blue square at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. But first, let me figure out how to collect the carried blue center that's inside the left blue square frame.
Actually, maybe the goal is just to collect all three blue centers by pressing SPACE at each blue square position. The carried blue centers move with the player inside the white extension. When I collected the right blue center, it moved into the white extension. When I collected the left blue center, it also moved into the white extension. But the carried blue center from the right square is now at cols 25-26, which is outside the white extension (cols 16-20). So maybe the carried blue centers don't all stay in the white extension.
Hmm, this is getting confusing. Let me just focus on collecting the third blue center (center blue square at rows 36-39, cols 32-35).
The player is at rows 28-31, cols 16-23 (white at 16-20, green at 21-23). The center blue square is at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. I need to move DOWN and RIGHT to reach it.
But first, I need to figure out the player's facing direction. The white extension is on the LEFT (cols 16-20), so the player is facing LEFT. To collect the center blue square, I need the player to be to the LEFT of it, facing RIGHT (white extension on the right side).
Let me try moving RIGHT to flip the player's direction, then navigate to the center blue square.
Actually, let me first try to understand the flipping mechanic better. When the player moved LEFT past the left blue square frame, the white extension flipped from the right side to the left side. So maybe the white extension flips when the player moves past a blue square frame in the opposite direction.
Let me just try moving RIGHT to see if the white extension flips back to the right side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 28-31, cols 16-23 (white at 16-20, green at 21-23)
# White extension on LEFT, player facing LEFT
# Left blue center collected (inside white extension at cols 17-18)
# Carried blue center from right square at cols 25-26 (inside left blue square frame)
# Center blue square at rows 36-39, cols 32-35
#
# Need to navigate to center blue square
# First let me move RIGHT to flip the player's direction

result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT x3:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
